title: Recursive Retrieval
concept: Following references within a retrieved chunk (links, citations, related sections) to pull in connected context on demand.

exercises.prompt: In the previous lesson, we learned about Sentence-Window Retrieval. What does it work. What is its main purpose?

options.text: It fetches a single target sentence and expands it to include surrounding sentences.
It searches for sentences that contain the word 'window'.
It splits every document into exactly two-sentence paragraphs.

exercises.explain: Sentence-window retrieval fetches a single highly relevant sentence and expands the context window to include its immediate neighbors.

cards.title: Following the Breadcrumbs
cards.body: Sometimes, the answer to a question isn't in one neat block. It's spread across documents like a trail of breadcrumbs. Recursive retrieval follows these clues automatically so your LLM doesn't get lost.

cards.title: What is Recursive Retrieval?
cards.body: Recursive retrieval is a technique where the search engine retrieves an initial document chunk, identifies references or links within it, and automatically fetches those connected chunks to compile a complete answer.

cards.title: The Wikipedia Surfer
cards.body: Imagine reading a Wikipedia article. Instead of printing the whole encyclopedia, you start with one paragraph. When you encounter a blue hyperlink, you instantly fetch and open that linked page to get the exact background details you need.

cards.title: Recursive Retrieval in Action
cards.body: A search for 'Section 4.1' returns a chunk saying 'See Appendix B for calculations'. The system detects this reference, automatically retrieves 'Appendix B', and feeds both chunks to the LLM.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Chunks Are Better
cards.body: People think retrieving larger text chunks is always better. In truth, huge chunks introduce noise and dilute the LLM's attention. Recursive retrieval keeps chunks small but connects them smartly.

cards.title: Window vs. Recursive
cards.body: Instead of grabbing a giant block of text, we pull a targeted starting point and follow its direct pointers.

left.text: Grabs a target sentence plus its immediate surrounding neighbor sentences.

right.text: Grabs a target chunk and jumps to linked sections or documents mentioned inside.

exercises.prompt: How does a recursive retrieval system decide what additional information to fetch?

options.text: It translates the query into five different languages.
It looks for links or citations in the retrieved text and fetches them.
It deletes the original query to save memory space.
It formats the entire output as a Python dictionary.

exercises.explain: Recursive retrieval is designed to dynamically follow links, citations, or pointers inside a document to fetch related context.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Recursive retrieval dynamically follows pointers inside retrieved chunks during search time.
exercises.explain: Recursive retrieval is dynamic; it analyzes the content of the retrieved chunks to find references and fetch them on the fly.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of recursive retrieval's core mechanism.
exercises.sentence: To find connected information, recursive retrieval automatically follows references found inside the first retrieved chunk.
exercises.explain: Recursive retrieval relies on parsing and following specific references or links found inside the initially retrieved chunks.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents an AI agent using recursive retrieval?

options.text: The user searches for 'Data Privacy' and the system returns a list of 100 unrelated policy documents.
The system retrieves a chunk about 'User Consent' which mentions 'Appendix C'. It automatically detects 'Appendix C' and fetches it to add to the LLM's context.
A developer manually copies and pastes three different sections of a PDF into the prompt template before sending it to the model.

exercises.explain: Option B represents an automated, recursive retrieval process where the system dynamically parses the retrieved text for references (Appendix C) and fetches them without manual user intervention.

exercises.prompt: You are building a research assistant that needs to answer complex questions about legal contracts. How does recursive retrieval help the LLM find information hidden across multiple cross-referenced sections?

options.text: It retrieves every single document in the database to be absolutely sure nothing is missed.
It extracts embedded references from the initial chunk and automatically fetches those linked chunks.
It randomly swaps words in the user query to see if different documents match.
It strictly limits the search to the first three words of the user's original query.

exercises.explain: Recursive retrieval dynamically parses the initial text for explicit references or child nodes, and recursively runs queries or fetches those specific targets to build a complete, highly relevant context window without bloat.
